elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents a quantum field theoretical formalism for studying magnons in finite nanostructures with arbitrary shapes and spatially nonuniform ground states.
elements.sent-2411-002: It extends the classical micromagnetic formalism by introducing a micromagnetic Hamiltonian quantum operator, which incorporates exchange, Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya, anisotropy, magnetostatic, and Zeeman energies.
elements.sent-2411-003: The nonuniformity of the ground state is handled by pointwise aligning the quantization axis of the magnetization field operator with the classical ground state.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Hamiltonian is expanded in the large spin-number limit and truncated to retain only terms quadratic in the components of the magnetization operator transverse to the quantization axis.
elements.sent-2411-005: This quadratic Hamiltonian is used to derive the linear quantum Landau-Lifshitz equation.
elements.sent-2411-006: By diagonalizing this equation under appropriate boundary and normalization conditions, a discrete set of magnon creation and annihilation operators is obtained, enabling a complete description of the magnon spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, the theory is applied to study the effects of temperature and shape on low-temperature thermal equilibrium fluctuations of magnons in thin ferromagnetic nanodisks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Magnons are quasiparticles associated to quantized spin waves, that are collective excitations of coupled spins in magnetic solids \cite{Bloch_1930, Bloch_1932, Holstein_Primakoff_1940, Herring_Kittel_1951, Dyson_1956, Kittel_book_1963, Akhiezer_book, Stancil_Prabhakar_book, Sparks_book .}
elements.sent-2411-009: They play a crucial role in the study of ferromagnetic materials influencing the thermodynamic properties of the media and controlling the dynamics of magnetization as a response to various external stimuli, such as microwave fields\cite{ Stancil_Prabhakar_book , spin-transfer-torque\cite{Urazhdin_2014 and spin-orbit effects\cite{Demidov_2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The study of magnons generation, manipulation, and control has given rise to the field of magnonics\cite{Lenk_2011, Barman_2021 , which focuses on the use of spin waves in magnetic nanostructures.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Magnonics intersects with other key nanotechnological research areas, such as spintronics\cite{Chumak_2015 , nanophotonics\cite{Chernov_2020 , and plasmonics\cite{Maksymov_2016 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In recent decades, the growing interest in quantum science and technology has also led to the use of magnons as carriers of quantum information, resulting in the emerging field of quantum magnonics\cite{Yuan2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The quantum theory of spin waves has a long history, originating from the seminal work of Bloch, Holstein, Primakoff, Kittel and Dyson\cite{Bloch_1930, Bloch_1932, Holstein_Primakoff_1940, Herring_Kittel_1951, Dyson_1956, Kittel_book_1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The formalism they developed, based on the concept of plane waves, remains widely used today.
elements.sent-2411-015: While this approach works well for bulk crystals with spatially uniform ground states and negligible boundary effects, it is less suitable for finite magnetic nanostructures.
elements.sent-2411-016: In finite systems, even when material properties are spatially homogeneous, spatially nonuniform (noncollinear) ground states often arise due to demagnetizing fields at the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-017: Additionally, spatial nonuniformities can result from Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya interactions\cite{Dzyaloshinskii1958, Moriya1960, Bogdanov1994 , which may lead to the formation of magnetic Skyrmions\cite{Fert2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we introduce a formalism in which the role of plane waves is replaced by the magnetization normal modes.
elements.sent-2411-019: The proposed approach consists in the quantum extension of classical micromagnetics, that is the continuum theory for ferromagnetism \cite{Brown_1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: It is based on a general quantum Hamiltonian that incorporates all relevant interactions, including exchange, Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya (DM), dipolar, anisotropy, and Zeeman effects.
elements.sent-2411-021: The formalism enables the self-consistent derivation of the magnon spectrum associated with magnetization normal modes, while fully accounting for boundary conditions and noncollinear ground states.
elements.sent-2411-022: The classical (non-quantum) treatment of normal modes in finite structures has traditionally relied on micromagnetic theory.
elements.sent-2411-023: A seminal contribution in this area came from Walker\cite{Walker_1957 , who analytically derived magnetostatic normal modes for spheroids.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Walker's approach was later extended to ellipsoidal geometries and further refined to include exchange interactions\cite{Aharoni_1963, Arias_2001, Arias_2004, Arias_2005a, Arias_2005b .}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, this analytical treatment was limited to cases involving spatially uniform ground states (saturated samples).
elements.sent-2411-026: On the other hand, extensive classical studies of dipole-exchange normal modes in ferromagnets with noncollinear ground states and arbitrary shapes have been conducted using numerical methods, including finite difference and finite element techniques \cite{Labbe_1999, Vukadinovic_200, Grimsditch_2004a, Grimsditch_2004b, Rivkin_2004, dAquino_2009, McMicheal_2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Notably, the numerical methods presented in Ref.\cite{dAquino_2009 are developed from a general operator-based formulation of the continuum classical normal modes problem, which is connected to the quantum treatment in the present paper.}
elements.sent-2411-028: General aspects of the quantum extension of classical micromagnetic theory were explored by Mills\cite{Mills_2006 ,\cite{Mills_2007 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: His formalism incorporates the finite size of the system through appropriate boundary conditions but is based on the simplifying assumption of spatially uniform ground states.
elements.sent-2411-030: On the other hand, noncollinear ground states in magnetic systems have been extensively studied within the context of Heisenberg-type spin models.
elements.sent-2411-031: Foundational work in this area dates back to the 1950s, particularly in relation to antiferromagnets, spiral, and helical magnets (see Ref. \cite{Rastelli_2013 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Over the past two decades, interest in materials exhibiting noncollinear magnetic ground states has resurged, in the field of magnetic nanotechnologies \cite{Diep_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent investigations in this area have examined Heisenberg and Haldane-gap spin rings \cite{Schuetz_2003, Schuetz_2004a, Schuetz_2004b , quantum antiferromagnets \cite{Spremo_2005, Kreisel_2008 , quantum spin waves in finite chiral spin chains \cite{Roldan-Molina_2014 , quantum fluctuations around skyrmionic ground states \cite{Roldan-Molina_2015 , and spin currents and spin superfluidity \cite{Rueckriegel_2017 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: These studies often rely on the assumption, used also in the present paper, that the system operates under conditions where quantum and thermal fluctuations are sufficiently small, justifying the treatment of low-energy quantum excitations as occurring near the classical ground state.
elements.sent-2411-035: Based on this assumption, the quantization procedure uses the direction of the classical noncollinear ground state as the quantization axis for the spin (or magnetization) quantum operators\cite{Rastelli_2013 , \cite{Haraldsen_2009 .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The quantum extension of the micromagnetic formalism presented in this paper is structured around two primary novel aspects.
elements.sent-2411-037: First, we introduce a formulation for analyzing magnon spectrum using the most general form of the Hamiltonian, rather than tailoring it to specific material properties or geometries, as is commonly done in the studies on the subject \cite{Rastelli_2013 -\cite{Rueckriegel_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The Hamiltonian operator considered, essentially the quantum version of the micromagnetic energy functional \cite{Brown_1963, Aharoni_Ferromagnetism , incorporates all relevant interactions among spins within a magnetic body of arbitrary shape, explicitly accounting for the often-overlooked nonlocal contributions of magnetostatic fields.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Notably, these fields are recognized as a significant source of computational complexity when addressing the diagonalization problem.
elements.sent-2411-040: Second, the diagonalization procedure is based on the quantum version of the Landau-Lifshitz equation linearized around the ground state \cite{Stancil_Prabhakar_book , \cite{Brown_1963 .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This approach, while equivalent to the conventional method based on the combination of Holstein-Primakoff transformation and Bogoliubov diagonalization \cite{Holstein_Primakoff_1940 , \cite{Sparks_book , has the advantage of maintaining boundary conditions in their simplest form.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The resulting diagonalization procedure gives rise to a generalized eigenvalue problem similar to those traditionally encountered in classical micromagnetic analyses \cite{dAquino_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This connection allows for the application of well-established micromagnetic computational techniques \cite{Abert_2019 and provides guidance on how to extend numerical codes developed for the classical case to the quantum scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In this context, an important aspect that arises is the normalization conditions that must be imposed on eigenfunctions describing the spatial profile of quantum magnons to preserve canonical commutation relations.
elements.sent-2411-045: The proposed formalism leads to an extension of Walker-type normalization conditions, as discussed in Refs.
elements.sent-2411-046: The effectiveness of the proposed theory is demonstrated in the final part of the paper, where numerical calculations of thermal equilibrium averages associated with the Cartesian components of magnetization in a thin disk are presented.
elements.sent-2411-047: For an in-plane magnetized film, the computations reveal that at low temperatures, edge magnons produce thermal fluctuations near the material boundaries that are significantly larger than those predicted by the plane-wave analysis for an infinitely in-plane magnetized thin film.
elements.sent-2411-048: In the case of a thin disk with a Skyrmion ground state, this approach enables the analysis of the spatially nonuniform distribution of thermal fluctuations, showing that these fluctuations are particularly enhanced in regions with the steepest gradients in the ground-state magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-049: These calculations, which can be applied to bodies of arbitrary shapes and noncollinear ground states, demonstrate the formalism's ability to study thermal equilibrium magnon populations.
elements.sent-2411-050: This capability is crucial for engineering magnetic nanostructures for low-temperature quantum magnonics applications in quantum computing and information processing \cite{Yuan2022 , \cite{Yuan2022a .}}

elements.sent-2411-053: Section \ref{sec:Diagonalization of Quantum Magnetization Dynamics is dedicated to the procedure for determining the magnon spectrum, establishing the conditions for diagonalizing the equation of motion, and introducing creation-annihilation operators to describe magnetization dynamics via magnonic quasi-particles.}

elements.sent-2411-057: We begin by considering a ferromagnetic Heisenberg-type model and proceed by taking its continuum limit, following the approach outlined in Refs.\,\cite{Kittel_book_1963 , \cite{Akhiezer_book .}}

elements.section-2411-007: Computations of thermal equilibrium averages in thin disks
elements.sent-2411-174: In this section, we want to present sample calculations that demonstrate the capability of the proposed formalism.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our focus will be on the low-temperature thermal equilibrium averages of observables associated with the Cartesian components of $\hat{\Mvec (\xvec)$.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Such thermal equilibrium computations are particularly relevant for initializing or engineering magnon populations in magnetic nanostructures, and this is important for low-temperature applications of quantum magnonics in quantum computing and information processing \cite{Yuan2022 , \cite{Yuan2022a .}}
elements.sent-2411-177: In the calculations, we consider the diagonalized magnon Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq:cond_diag_H_canonical_c_bis} \hat{H}_2^\prime = \sum_{m \in N^* }{ \hbar \omega_m} \hat{c}_m^\dag \hat{c}_m = \sum_{m \in N^* }{ \hbar \omega_m} \hat{n}_m\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\hat{n}_m = \hat{c}_m^\dag \hat{c}_m \, ,\end{equation} is the number operator of magnons with frequency $\omega_m$.
elements.sent-2411-178: The Hamiltonian \eqref{eq:cond_diag_H_canonical_c_bis is obtained from \eqref{eq:cond_diag_H_canonical_c after removing the zero-point energy.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: The zero-point contribution in field theory of magnons is actually infinite as the spectrum is unbounded from above, similarly to what happens in quantum electrodynamics \cite{Heitler_1936 .}
elements.sent-2411-180: In addition, we are here only concerned with the temperature-dependent contribution to the quantities under investigation, which does not include the zero-point energy.
elements.sent-2411-181: For a generic observable quantity $\hat{F}$, we define the equilibrium thermal average as \begin{equation}\label{eq:thermal_averages} \langle \hat{F} \rangle_T = tr(\hat{\rho}_eq \hat{F}) \, ,\end{equation} where $\hat{\rho _\text{eq}$ is the equilibrium density operator given by \begin{equation}\hat{\rho}_eq = \frac{1}{Z}\exp(-\beta \hat{H}_2^\prime)\end{equation} where $\beta = 1/(k_B T)$, $k_B = 1.380649 \cdot 10^{-23}$ J/K is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ the absolute temperature and $Z = \mathrm{tr (\exp(-\beta \hat{H _2'))$ is the partition function.}}}

elements.sent-2411-230: This work introduces a quantum-field-based micromagnetic approach for deriving the magnon spectrum in finite nanostructures.
elements.sent-2411-231: The derivation applies to a general quantum micromagnetic Hamiltonian that includes all relevant interactions, such as isotropic and anisotropic exchange, Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya (DM) interaction, dipolar interactions, anisotropy, and Zeeman effects.
elements.sent-2411-232: This formalism is well-suited for addressing spatially nonuniform ground states and accurately accounts for boundary effects.
elements.sent-2411-233: The computations presented in the paper demonstrate that edge effects induce quantitative changes in magnetization dynamics that cannot be captured by plane wave formalism alone.
elements.sent-2411-234: The proposed approach extends the classical micromagnetic framework into the quantum regime.
elements.sent-2411-235: This advancement is expected to be of interest to researchers using classical micromagnetics to model systems and devices in fields like magnonics, spintronics, and related areas, particularly those interested in incorporating quantum effects into their models.
elements.sent-2411-236: This paper represents an initial step in this direction by addressing the case of non-interacting magnons.
elements.sent-2411-237: The framework established here can be extended in multiple directions.
elements.sent-2411-238: Key examples include the quantum treatment of resonance under both transverse and longitudinal pumping, a perturbative analysis of magnon scattering with higher-order terms in the Hamiltonian expansion, and the study of relaxation processes due to interactions with a thermal reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-239: These topics will be explored in future studies, building on the theory of free magnon dynamics established here.

elements.sent-2411-243: The quantum Hamiltonian \eqref{eq:magnetization_hamiltonian can be written in the following form: \begin{equation}\label{eq:H_in_terms_of_W} H[\hat{\Mvec}] = W[\hat{\Mvec},\hat{\Mvec}] - \int_\Omega \mu_0\hat{\Mvec}\cdot \Hveca dV \, ,\end{equation} where $W[\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,]$ is a bilinear form associated to quadratic terms of the Hamiltonian \eqref{eq:magnetization_hamiltonian and it is given by the following formula \begin{equation}\nonumber {W}[\hat{\Fvec}, \hat{\Gvec}] = \int_\Omega dV \left\{ \frac{A_{kh}^{uv}}{M_0^2}\, \frac{\partial \hat{F}^u}{\partial x_k}\frac{\partial \hat{G}^v}{\partial x_h} +\right. \nonumber \left. \qquad\qquad\qquad + \frac{1}{2}{ \frac{D_{k}^{uv}}{M_0^2} } \left( \! \hat{ F}^u \frac{\partial \hat{G}^v}{\partial x_k} - \frac{\partial \hat{F}^u}{\partial x_k} \hat{ G}^v \!\right)+ \right. \label{eq:magnetization_hamiltonian_bilinear_quantum_0} \left. \qquad\qquad\qquad \quad - \,\hat{F}^u \frac{K^{uv}}{M_0^2}\hat{G}^v - \frac{\mu_0}{2}\hat{\Fvec }\cdot \Hvecm[\hat{\Gvec}] \right\} \, ,\end{equation} where $\hat{\Fvec (\xvec)$ and $\hat{\Gvec (\xvec)$ are two generic quantum fields the components of which do not necessarily commute.}}}}

elements.section-2411-010: Quantum spin wave in infinite thin film using the plane wave formalism.
elements.sent-2411-274: In this appendix we study the limiting case of the problems treated in Sec.\,\ref{sec:applications in which the ferromagnetic body is treated as an infinite ultra-thin film of thickness $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-275: We consider the two cases: in the first case the thin-film is saturated by an in-plane applied field $H_a$ along the $x$-axis, and in the second case the film is magnetized in the $z$-direction (perpendicular) to the film due to the presence of perpendicular anisotropy.
elements.sent-2411-276: As we did in Sec.\,\ref{sec:applications , we assume that exchange interaction is isotropic, and (when it is present) that asisotropy is uniaxial with anisotropy asis perpedicular to the film plane.}
elements.sent-2411-278: As in Sec.\,\ref{sec:applications , the magnetization field is assumed to not vary across the thickness of the film.}
elements.sent-2411-279: The derivation of the magnon spectrum can be carried out analytically using plane waves formalism,based on the expansion \eqref{eq:plane_waves_diag_H_1 , with an in-plane wave vector: \begin{equation}\kvec = k_x \evec_x + k_y \evec_y\, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-280: The analytical results of plane wave formalism can be used as reference for the case of the disk with finite diameter.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Spin wave derivation in the case of in-plane magnetized thin film
elements.sent-2411-281: In this case, we assume that no DM neither anisotropy terms are present, this means that $D_k^{uv =0$, $K_1=0$.}

elements.sent-2411-283: The applied field is spatially uniform and applied along the $x$-direction, i.e. $\Hveca = H_a \evec_x$.
elements.sent-2411-284: The magnon spectrum is continuous and the associated frequencies can be expressed in terms of the dispersion relation \begin{equation}\omega(\kvec) = \gamma \mu_0 M_0 \sqrt{D_{0\bot}^{yy}(\kvec) D_{0\bot}^{zz}(\kvec)} \, ,\end{equation} where $D^{yy _{0\bot (\kvec), D^{zz _{0\bot (\kvec)$ are given by the formulas \begin{equation}D^{yy}_{0\bot}(\kvec) = \frac{2A}{\mu_0 M_0^2}k^2 + \lambda_0 + [1 - S(kd)]\frac{k_y^2}{k^2}\, , D^{zz}_{0\bot}(\kvec) = \frac{2A}{\mu_0 M_0^2}k^2 + \lambda_0 + S(kd)\, ,\end{equation} and where \begin{equation}\label{eq:def_S_k_lambda_0} k = \sqrt{k_x^2+k_y^2} \,, \,\, S(kd) = \frac{1- e^{-kd}}{kd} \, , \,\, \lambda_0 = \frac{H_a}{M_0} .\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-286: These are given by the following formulas: \begin{equation}\nonumber \langle (\hat{M}_\bot^z(x,y))^2 \rangle_T = \label{eq:inf_thin_fil_mq_mz} \quad \frac{\mu_0 \hbar (\gamma M_0)^2}{(2\pi)^2d} \int_{R^2} dk_x dk_y \left\{ \langle \hat{n}(\kvec) \rangle_T \frac{D^{yy}_{0\bot}(\kvec)}{\omega(\kvec)} \right\} \, ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\nonumber \langle (\hat{M}^y_\bot(x,y))^2 \rangle_T = \label{eq:inf_thin_fil_mq_my} \quad \frac{\mu_0 \hbar (\gamma M_0)^2}{(2\pi)^2d} \int_{R^2} dk_x dk_y \left\{ \langle \hat{n}(\kvec) \rangle_T \frac{D^{zz}_{0\bot}(\kvec)}{\omega(\kvec)} \right\} \, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\langle \hat{n}(\kvec) \rangle_T = \frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega(\kvec)}-1} \, .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-289: Here, for comparison with the second set of numerical computations presented in Sec.\,\ref{sec:applications , we report the formulas for the case of infinite thin film that admits a spatially uniform ground state in the $z$-direction, $\Mvec_0 = M_0 \evec_z$ caused by a perpendicular anisotropy that is defined by a tensor $K^{uv}$ in which the only nonzero entry is $K^{zz = K_1$.}}

elements.sent-2411-291: In the present case, the continuous magnon spectrum is characterized by the dispersion relation \begin{equation}\label{eq:dispersion_rel_perp} \omega(\kvec) = \gamma \mu_0 M_0 \sqrt{D_{0\bot}^{xx}(\kvec) D_{0\bot}^{yy}(\kvec)- (D_{0\bot}^{xy}(\kvec))^2 } \, ,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:D0botkvec_xx} D^{uv}_{0\bot}(\kvec) = \left( \frac{2A k^2}{\mu_0 M_0^2} + \lambda_0\right) \delta_{uv} + [1 - S(kd)]\frac{k_u k_v}{k^2}\, ,\end{equation} where $u,v=x,y$ ($D^{uv _{0\bot (\kvec)$ is $2\times2$ matrix), $k$ and $S(kd)$ are as defined Eq.\eqref{eq:def_S_k_lambda_0 , and where \begin{equation}\lambda_0 = {2K_1}/({\mu_0 M_0^2}) - 1\,.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-293: Next, we compute the the thermal equilibrium mean square values of $x$ and $y$ components of $\hat{\Mvec (x,y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Due to the rotational symmetry of the problem, this two averages are the same and are given by the following formula: \begin{equation}\nonumber \langle (\hat{M}_\bot^x(x,y))^2 \rangle_T = \langle (\hat{M}^y_\bot(x,y))^2 \rangle_T = \label{eq:inf_thin_fil_mq_mz_perp} \frac{\mu_0 \hbar (\gamma M_0)^2}{2(2\pi)^2d} \int_{R^2} dk_x dk_y \left\{ \langle \hat{n}(\kvec) \rangle_T \frac{tr(D^{uv}_{0\bot}(\kvec))}{\omega(\kvec)} \right\} \, ,\end{equation} where $\text{tr (\,\cdot\,)$ denotes the trace of a tensor.}
elements.sent-2411-295: Also in this case the invariance of the system with respect to in-plane spatial translations, leads to results that are not function of the position.
